source.title: Psychological and neural mechanisms of the affective dimension of pain

confidence_rationale: Canonical formulation of the sensory-discriminative vs affective-motivational dissociation. Direct anatomical support (ACC/AIC vs S1/S2), hypnotic-dissociation evidence, and two-stage model (pain unpleasantness -> secondary affective response) directly motivate the dual-stream substrate ree-v3 already implements. High source quality, high mapping fidelity; slight transfer risk because the paper treats affective pain as a subjective report rather than an action-selection signal.
failure_signatures: Models that collapse sensory and affective pain into a single scalar cost will fail to predict the clinical dissociation (cingulotomy patients still detect noxious stimuli but no longer rate them as unpleasant).

Affective pain in Price's two-stage model is at least partly generated post-hoc from secondary appraisal (harm implications, suffering expectations) -- a consumer that only reads instantaneous z_harm_a and never updates it with trajectory-level consequence inference would underweight the very signal the theory says matters.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Pain has two functionally and anatomically dissociable dimensions: sensory-discriminative (intensity/location/quality, subserved by lateral thalamus -> S1/S2) and affective-motivational (unpleasantness, suffering, action urgency, subserved by medial thalamus -> ACC/AIC). The affective dimension further splits into immediate pain unpleasantness and secondary appraisal-based affect (suffering, threat to future wellbeing).
mapping.ree_translation: SD-011's dual z_harm_s / z_harm_a split correctly instantiates the primary sensory/affective dissociation. But Price's secondary-appraisal stage -- where affective pain is re-generated from inferred future consequences -- implies z_harm_a should not be purely bottom-up from current noxious input; it should also receive a top-down signal from trajectory-level harm forecasting. This directly speaks to Option B (forward-rollout affective cost) over pure Option A (read-off-current-latent).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Price's paper predates the computational-decision framing entirely. It maps affective pain onto subjective unpleasantness, not explicitly onto action-value computations. The bridge to action selection requires the Baliki/Seymour literature -- Price alone does not specify how z_harm_a should reach the policy.
mapping.source_context: Review synthesizing human psychophysics, hypnotic-suggestion fMRI, and cingulotomy/patient data.

confidence_components.notes: Foundational review, not original empirical work, but cited as canonical. Mapping to ree-v3 is strong for the dual-stream existence claim; weaker for the specific wiring question because the paper does not speak the language of action-selection cost functions.
